agent1_snippets.title: HIV and AIDS -- Treatment / Management -- Recommended Therapy for Treatment-Naive Patients
agent1_snippets.content: Current guidelines recommend initiating INSTI-based therapy with a dual NRTI backbone before conducting further laboratory testing for most patients with HIV. In select cases where the HIV RNA level is <500,000 copies/mL, no coinfection with hepatitis B occurs, and no genotypic resistance is present, a 2-drug regimen with dolutegravir and lamivudine can be considered first-line. Tenofovir plus lamivudine or emtricitabine are the preferred NRTIs, and bictegravir and dolutegravir are the preferred INSTIs due to their effectiveness, adverse effect profiles, shorter duration to virological suppression, and lower propensity to develop resistance. INSTI-based regimens achieve faster viral suppression compared to protease inhibitor or NNRTI-containing regimens. The addition of other agents in the setting of advanced HIV on presentation does not improve clinical outcomes at the onset of treatment and is not recommended. Abacavir is no longer recommended as initial therapy due to the association with cardiovascular disease, the risk of hypersensitivity, and the need for HLA B*5701 testing. Antiretroviral treatment should be started regardless of the CD4+ count to reduce a combination of serious AIDS- and non–AIDS-related events and death from any cause if started at diagnosis compared to a deferred initiation of treatment. [24] [25] [26] [27] [28]

agent1_snippets.title: HIV Antiretroviral Therapy -- Continuing Education Activity
agent1_snippets.content: A typical initial HIV regimen includes 3 HIV medications from a minimum of 2 drug classes. Although this treatment is not curative, it can provide longer lives for patients and reduce HIV transmission. This reduction of transmission has become a popular use of antiretroviral therapy for HIV-positive individuals with an HIV-negative partner. The successes of antiretroviral therapy have reduced HIV to a chronic condition in many parts of the world as progression to AIDS has become rare. Studies have found that the 3-drug regimen has led to a 60% to 80% decline in rates of AIDs, hospitalization, and death. By 2030, the CDC plans to implement a 90-90-90 plan (90% HIV diagnosed, 90% on therapy, and 90% suppressed). This activity describes the indications, contraindications, and use of HIV antiretroviral therapy and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in promoting their safety.

agent1_snippets.title: HIV Prevention -- Introduction
agent1_snippets.content: Targets and initiatives set by the United Nations (UN), the WHO, and individual countries' centers for disease control have fostered immense progress in scaling up testing and implementing antiretroviral therapy (ART. However, knowledge of HIV status, use of preventive services, and access to and retention in care remain suboptimal in the United States and worldwide, with large inequities between populations. The Joint UN Programme for HIV/AIDS (UNAIDS) has set "95-95-95" goals for 2025. 95% of all individuals with HIV will know their status 95% of all those who have HIV will receive antiretroviral therapy 95% of all people on ART will achieve viral suppression The projected impact of this will be fewer than 370,000 people acquiring HIV and fewer than 250,000 people dying from HIV in 2025 (see Image. Global Trends in people acquiring or dying from HIV).

agent1_snippets.title: HIV and AIDS -- Introduction
agent1_snippets.content: HIV imposes high costs on both patients and the healthcare system. Infection with HIV increases the risk of chronic disease, particularly cardiac and neurological. Although ART delays disease progress, treatment does not cure HIV, causes adverse effects, and requires consistent, prolonged connection to the healthcare system. Several barriers to universal treatment exist, including public- and self-stigma, lack of adequate access to care, inappropriate care, and costs. Using local clinical guidelines for managing HIV improves patient outcomes and prevents HIV transmission. Clinical guidelines promote quality programming for prompt diagnosis, treatment, and connection to care for patients with or at risk of acquiring HIV. Increasing the involvement of community-led organizations in HIV testing and treatment and integrating medical services for related health issues extend the reach of precise services, improve linkage to care, and improve overall health. Supportive social and policy environments regarding access to services, screening, reporting test results, and discrimination can safeguard patients and the community.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1649
agent2_snippets.content: Non-nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) constitute a class of antiretroviral drugs that inhibit HIV reverse transcriptase by binding to a distinct site on the enzyme, causing conformational changes that block viral DNA synthesis. Both efavirenz and etravirine are identified as members of the Non-nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors class, and nevirapin is also recognized as a member of this same class. Recognizing that efavirenz, etravirine, and nevirapin all belong to the Non-nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors group aids clinicians in selecting appropriate HIV treatment regimens and in anticipating class‑related efficacy and adverse‑effect profiles.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 670
agent2_snippets.content: Non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) constitute an antiretroviral drug class that includes doravirine, delavirdin, efavirenz, etravirine and nevirapine. These agents share a common mechanism of inhibiting the reverse transcriptase enzyme of HIV, and they are grouped together because of their similar pharmacologic profile. Among them, doravirine has a specific drug‑interaction warning: it is contraindicated when used with rifapentine, a member of a class of rifamycin antibiotics, due to the risk of reduced efficacy or increased toxicity. Recognizing the class membership of these NNRTIs and the contraindication between doravirine and rifapentine is essential for safe prescribing and optimal management of patients receiving antiretroviral therapy.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 277
agent2_snippets.content: Nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) are a class of antiretroviral agents that target the HIV reverse transcriptase enzyme, thereby preventing viral DNA synthesis and replication. This drug class includes tenofovir disoproxil, dideoxycytidine, emtricitabine, lamivudine, stavudine, and zidovudine, each of which functions as a nucleoside or nucleotide analogue that is incorporated into the growing viral DNA chain and causes termination. Recognizing that these specific medications are members of the NRTI class is essential for constructing effective combination antiretroviral regimens, optimizing therapeutic outcomes, and managing class‑related toxicities in patients living with HIV.

agent2_snippets.content: HIV is a chronic viral infection that can be managed with antiretroviral therapy, and tenofovir disoproxil is an antiretroviral prodrug that may be used to treat HIV infection. agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 105
agent2_snippets.content: HIV infection is primarily transmitted through shared needle use, and its prevention and treatment rely on a broad spectrum of antiretroviral agents that target the viral reverse transcriptase enzyme. Nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) – including tenofovir disoproxil, emtricitabine (also referenced as emtricitabin), lamivudine (lamivudin), stavudine (stavudin), zidovudine (zidovudin), and dideoxycytidine – act as nucleoside analogues that are incorporated into viral DNA and cause premature chain termination, forming a core component of combination HIV therapy. Non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) – doravirine, delavirdine (and its mesylate formulation), efavirenz, etravirine, nevirapine, and rilpivirine – bind to a distinct site on reverse transcriptase to inhibit viral replication. Doravirine has a specific contraindication when co‑administered with the rifamycin antibiotic rifapentine, reflecting a clinically important drug‑class interaction. Understanding the class membership, mechanisms of action, preventive potential, and contraindications of these NRTI and NNRTI agents is essential for optimizing patient care, selecting effective regimens, managing drug interactions, and reducing the spread of HIV.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 73
agent2_snippets.content: Direct‑acting antivirals (DAAs) encompass several pharmacologic classes used to treat viral infections, including HIV; within DAAs, cyclic amines such as rimantadin alter cellular synthetic activity, while integrase inhibitors, non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) and nucleoside/nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) constitute additional subclasses. NRTIs include abacavir (synonymous with dideoxyadenosine), dideoxycytidine, emtricitabine, lamivudine, stavudine, zidovudine, and tenofovir disoproxil, and are employed both for HIV prevention (e.g., abacavir, emtricitabine, lamivudine) and therapy. NNRTIs comprise delavirdin, delavirdin‑mesylate, efavirenz, etravirine, nevirapine, doravirine, rilpivirine, and raltegravir‑type agents that bind distinct sites on reverse transcriptase; doravirine is contraindicated with rifampicin and rilpivirine with pantoprazole due to drug‑drug interaction risks. Integrase inhibitors—dolutegravir, elvitegravir, raltegravir, cabotegravir, and fostemsavir—block the viral integrase enzyme required for proviral integration. Fixed‑dose combination regimens such as abacavir/dolutegravir/lamivudine and abacavir/lamivudine (also representing dideoxyadenosine/lamivudine) are classified as “Antivirals for treatment of HIV infections, combinations” and exemplify DAA‑based HIV therapy. Antiviral combinations specifically formulated for HIV are themselves a type of DAA. Shared needle use is identified as a direct risk factor for HIV acquisition, whereas methotrexate sodium is contraindicated in patients with HIV infection, underscoring important safety considerations in this population. Together, these entities delineate the classification, mechanisms of action, therapeutic and preventive roles, combination formulations, nomenclature equivalences, and contraindications relevant to comprehensive HIV management.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 1 -- Community 113
agent2_snippets.content: HIV infection is closely linked to the behavioral risk factor of shared needle use, underscoring the importance of harm‑reduction counseling in prevention strategies. A broad spectrum of antiretroviral agents demonstrates preventive activity against HIV acquisition, including delavirdin, delavirdin‑mesylate, dolutegravir, efavirenz, elvitegravir, emtricitabine, etravirine, lamivudine, nevirapine, raltegravir, rilpivirine, stavudine, zidovudine and tenofovir disoproxil. Cabotegravir is recognized for therapeutic use in established HIV infection. These drugs are organized into pharmacologic classes that reflect distinct mechanisms of action: non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) comprise doravirine, delavirdin, efavirenz, etravirine, rilpivirine and nevirapine, which bind to a specific site on HIV reverse transcriptase; nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) include dideoxycytidine, emtricitabine, lamivudine, stavudine, zidovudine and tenofovir disoproxil, acting as intracellularly phosphorylated chain terminators; and integrase inhibitors—cabotegravir, dolutegravir, elvitegravir and raltegravir—target viral DNA integration. Notable drug‑drug contraindications are identified within these classes: doravirine is contraindicated with rifapentine, and rilpivirine is contraindicated with pantoprazole, highlighting the need for careful medication reconciliation. Recognizing the relationships among needle‑sharing risk, the preventive and therapeutic roles of specific antiretrovirals, their class‑based mechanisms, and relevant contraindications is essential for constructing safe, effective HIV prophylaxis and treatment regimens and for optimizing patient counseling and care.

agent2_snippets.content: Nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors constitute a class of antiretroviral drugs that target the HIV reverse transcriptase enzyme, thereby blocking viral replication. This therapeutic class includes tenofovir disoproxil, emtricitabine, lamivudine, zidovudine, and dideoxycytidine (also known as zalcitabine). By sharing a common mechanism of action, these agents are frequently combined in HIV treatment regimens to achieve synergistic viral suppression and reduce the risk of resistance development.

agent2_snippets.content: Cabotegravir, elvitegravir and raltegravir are all members of the pharmacologic class identified as Integrase Inhibitors, antiinfectives for systematic use. As integrase inhibitors, each of these agents targets the HIV integrase enzyme, thereby preventing the integration of viral DNA into the host genome and serving as essential components of systemic antiretroviral therapy. Their shared classification underscores a common mechanism of action and therapeutic purpose in the treatment of HIV infection, highlighting the importance of this drug class in modern HIV management.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 135
agent2_snippets.content: HIV infection is strongly associated with shared needle use, which constitutes a major transmission route, and its prevention and treatment rely on antiretroviral agents classified by mechanism of action. Nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) target the HIV reverse transcriptase enzyme, mimic natural nucleosides, and cause chain termination, thereby inhibiting viral DNA synthesis. Key NRTIs include tenofovir disoproxil, abacavir, didanosine (dideoxycytidine), emtricitabine, lamivudine, stavudine, and zidovudine; each has demonstrated activity in pre‑exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) and in early or combination treatment regimens to suppress viral replication. The non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor delavirdine mesylate also possesses preventive properties by directly inhibiting reverse transcriptase. Integrase inhibitors—cabotegravir, dolutegravir, elvitegravir, and raltegravir—block the viral integrase enzyme, preventing integration of viral DNA into the host genome; cabotegravir is notable for its dual use in both treatment of established infection and PrEP, while the other agents are primarily employed for treatment but may be used preventively in high‑risk settings. Collectively, the correct classification and combined use of NRTIs, non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors, and integrase inhibitors form the backbone of modern HIV prevention strategies and therapeutic protocols, optimizing patient outcomes through viral suppression and reduced acquisition risk.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 1178
agent2_snippets.content: HIV infection is a viral disease that is closely linked to the practice of shared needle use, a major route of transmission among individuals who inject drugs. Because of the potential for adverse effects and drug interactions, methotrexate sodium is contraindicated in patients with HIV infection, meaning it should not be used in this population. Conversely, the antiretroviral agent delavirdine mesylate has been noted to possess preventive activity against HIV infection, offering a possible strategy for reducing the risk of acquiring the virus. Understanding these relationships—transmission through shared needles, the contraindication of methotrexate sodium, and the preventive potential of delavirdine mesylate—is essential for optimizing patient care and guiding therapeutic decisions in the context of HIV.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1228
agent2_snippets.content: Delavirdin, efavirenz and nevirapin are all members of the pharmacologic class known as non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs). NNRTIs act by binding to a specific allosteric site on the HIV reverse transcriptase enzyme, inducing conformational changes that inhibit viral DNA synthesis and thereby suppress HIV replication. Recognizing that delavirdin, efavirenz and nevirapin belong to this same drug class is important for clinicians because agents within the NNRTI class share similar mechanisms of action, resistance patterns, and potential side‑effect profiles, which influences the selection and management of antiretroviral therapy in patients living with HIV.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 131
agent2_snippets.content: Antivirals for treatment of HIV infections, combinations is a therapeutic class that includes the fixed‑dose regimens abacavir / dolutegravir / lamivudine and abacavir / lamivudine. Both combinations contain the nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor (NRTI) abacavir together with lamivudine, and the former also incorporates the integrase inhibitor dolutegravir. Abacavir, along with tenofovir disoproxil, dideoxycytidine, emtricitabine, lamivudine, stavudine and zidovudine, belongs to the broader pharmacologic group called nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors, agents that inhibit HIV reverse transcriptase by mimicking natural nucleosides. Chemically, abacavir is an analog of dideoxyadenosine, and this structural relationship is reflected in the mapping of abacavir, as well as the abacavir‑containing combination products (abacavir / dolutegravir / lamivudine and abacavir / lamivudine), to dideoxyadenosine. Recognizing these class memberships and chemical mappings aids clinicians in selecting appropriate antiretroviral regimens and anticipating cross‑resistance or toxicity patterns among NRTI‑based therapies.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 2 -- Community 1024
agent2_snippets.content: Non-nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) are a class of antiretroviral agents that inhibit the HIV reverse transcriptase enzyme by binding to a distinct site, thereby preventing viral replication. The NNRTI class includes the drugs efavirenz, etravirine, nevirapine, and rilpivirine, all of which share this mechanism of action. While NNRTIs are valuable components of combination HIV therapy, clinicians must consider potential drug‑drug interactions; for instance, the proton pump inhibitor pantoprazole is contraindicated with rilpivirine because it can impair rilpivirine absorption and reduce its therapeutic effectiveness. Awareness of these class relationships and contraindications is essential for optimizing patient care and achieving successful viral suppression.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 90
agent2_snippets.content: HIV infection is strongly associated with shared‑needle use, which constitutes a major route of viral transmission. A broad spectrum of antiretroviral agents has been identified for both prevention (pre‑exposure prophylaxis) and treatment of HIV infection. Preventive activity has been reported for delavirdin, delavirdin‑mesylate, dolutegravir, efavirenz, elvitegravir, emtricitabine, etravirine, lamivudine, nevirapine, raltegravir, rilpivirine, stavudine, zidovudine and tenofovir disoproxil, whereas cabotegravir is highlighted for its therapeutic effect in established infection. These drugs belong to three principal pharmacologic classes. Non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) – delavirdin, rilpivirine, efavirenz, etravirine and nevirapine – bind directly to the HIV reverse transcriptase enzyme and inhibit viral replication. Nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) – dideoxycytidine, emtricitabine, lamivudine, stavudine, zidovudine and tenofovir disoproxil – require intracellular phosphorylation and act as chain terminators of reverse transcription. Integrase inhibitors – cabotegravir, dolutegravir, elvitegravir and raltegravir – target the HIV integrase enzyme, blocking integration of viral DNA into the host genome. Understanding the transmission risk posed by shared‑needle use together with the preventive and therapeutic properties of these NNRTIs, NRTIs and integrase inhibitors, and their class‑specific mechanisms, informs comprehensive strategies for HIV prevention, combination antiretroviral therapy, resistance mitigation and optimal patient care.

agent2_snippets.content: HIV infection is closely associated with the practice of shared needle use, which represents a major route of transmission. Several antiretroviral agents have preventive activity against HIV infection, including delavirdine‑mesylate, dolutegravir, elvitegravir, and raltegravir. In addition to prevention, cabotegravir and fostemsavir are employed as therapeutic agents for the treatment of established HIV infection. Dolutegravir, elvitegravir, cabotegravir, and raltegravir are all members of the Integrase Inhibitors class of systemic anti‑infectives, reflecting their shared mechanism of targeting the viral integrase enzyme. This network of transmission risk, drug contraindications, preventive agents, and treatment options underscores the importance of integrated management strategies for individuals at risk for or living with HIV infection.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1685
agent2_snippets.content: Non-nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) are a class of antiretroviral drugs that inhibit the HIV reverse transcriptase enzyme by binding to a site distinct from the active site, thereby preventing viral replication. The NNRTI class includes the agents delavirdin, etravirine, and nevirapin, each of which shares this mechanism of action while differing in pharmacokinetic properties, resistance profiles, and clinical use. Delavirdin was one of the earlier NNRTIs and is administered three times daily, whereas etravirine is a newer, more potent NNRTI often used in treatment‑experienced patients due to its activity against certain resistant HIV strains. Nevirapin (commonly known as nevirapine) is widely used in both adult and pediatric regimens and is notable for its long half‑life and potential for hypersensitivity reactions. Recognizing that delavirdin, etravirine, and nevirapin all belong to the Non-nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor class helps clinicians select appropriate therapy, anticipate cross‑resistance, and manage side‑effect profiles in the comprehensive care of individuals living with HIV.
